text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- OUTPATIENT OFFICE VISIT
Department of Medical Oncology / Genitourinary Oncology Program

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
ATTENDING: [Medical Oncologist, Genitourinary Oncology]
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient -- Routine Follow-Up / Response Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Kowalski is a 64-year-old male with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation, currently receiving second-line cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily, presenting for routine follow-up visit at approximately 9 months of cabozantinib therapy (approximately 18 months from his initial diagnosis). He is here for symptom review, laboratory evaluation, and to plan his upcoming restaging CT scan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was originally diagnosed with Stage IV (cT3bN1M1) clear cell RCC of the right kidney approximately 18 months ago. At presentation, he had an 11.4 cm right renal mass with extension into the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (right para-aortic and aortocaval nodes), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. Genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) demonstrated VHL frameshift, PBRM1 and BAP1 co-loss (the latter associated with his higher grade and sarcomatoid features), SETD2 loss, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification, and TP53 gain-of-function mutation -- a genomically complex tumor. He is IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors: <1 year from diagnosis to systemic therapy, thrombocytosis at presentation).

He underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 15 months ago, with R0 resection of the primary and 3 of 8 retroperitoneal nodes positive for metastatic disease with sarcomatoid features. Sarcomatoid differentiation was revised to 22% of viable tumor volume on final nephrectomy specimen.

He then received first-line ipilimumab (1 mg/kg) plus nivolumab (3 mg/kg) every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks as maintenance. His course was complicated by immune-related hypothyroidism (grade 2) after cycle 2, managed with levothyroxine without need for steroid therapy or immunotherapy interruption. On restaging imaging, he demonstrated a discordant/mixed response: his pulmonary metastases showed clear improvement (the dominant right lower lobe nodule decreased from 1.4 cm to a nadir of approximately 0.7 cm), but he developed new and progressive retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease over the ensuing months. This pattern was felt to possibly reflect differential sensitivity between the conventional clear cell and the sarcomatoid components of his tumor. Despite a period of continued observation given the pulmonary response, progressive nodal disease was confirmed at approximately 9 months from initiation of systemic therapy, meeting criteria for overall disease progression on nivolumab.

He was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib, initiated at 60 mg daily. Within the first 3 weeks of cabozantinib, he developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (HFS) and grade 2 diarrhea, requiring dose reduction to 40 mg daily and initiation of supportive measures. He also developed grade 2 hypertension at approximately 5 weeks, requiring augmentation of his antihypertensive regimen with addition of amlodipine to his pre-existing lisinopril, which had already been dose-escalated in anticipation of TKI-related hypertension.

On first restaging at 3 months of cabozantinib (40 mg), he achieved a partial response in his nodal disease (approximately 27% reduction in sum of target nodal diameters). At 6 months of TKI therapy, his most recent full restaging showed stable disease overall, with all nodal and pulmonary disease remaining stable. A small, indeterminate sclerotic focus in the right iliac bone noted on that CT was subsequently assessed by whole-body bone scan, which was reassuring -- the focus was felt to be most consistent with a reactive/degenerative process without convincing evidence of osteoblastic or osteolytic metastatic disease.

He now presents for a scheduled routine visit at approximately 9 months of cabozantinib therapy. He has been on the drug continuously at the 40 mg daily dose since the dose reduction. He reports tolerating therapy acceptably and continues to work part-time. His primary concerns today relate to his ongoing chronic toxicities and desire for an update on his disease status.

Specific symptoms reviewed in detail:
- Hand-foot syndrome: Grade 1, chronic and stable at the current dose. He has adapted his daily routine to include diligent foot care: applying urea 20% cream to palms and soles twice daily, wearing well-cushioned footwear, avoiding prolonged standing. He denies active blistering or open skin breakdown. Mild erythema and toughening of the skin on the plantar surfaces persists but is not painful at rest.
- Diarrhea: Grade 1, intermittent. He reports 1-3 loose stools above his baseline on most days, but uses dietary modification (low-residue diet, avoiding high-fat meals) and takes PRN loperamide on days with more bothersome symptoms. He has not required regular daily loperamide in recent weeks.
- Dysgeusia: He notes persistent grade 1 taste changes that have been present for several months. Specifically, foods taste "metallic" or "flat." This has affected his appetite and the enjoyment of meals, but he reports no significant weight loss. He is making dietary accommodations, emphasizing foods with stronger, more neutral flavors.
- Fatigue: Grade 1 fatigue, stable and manageable. He paces his activities and reports adequate sleep. Denies profound asthenia or inability to perform activities of daily living.
- Hypertension: Blood pressure well-controlled on current regimen (lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 10 mg daily). He monitors at home and reports most readings in the 130-140/80-85 range. No headaches, visual changes, or other hypertensive symptoms.
- Edema: He reports complete resolution of the bilateral lower extremity edema that was present at time of disease progression on nivolumab. No recurrence.
- No chest pain, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
- No new bone pain, no neurological symptoms.
- No evidence of hepatotoxicity symptoms (no jaundice, no abdominal pain, no dark urine).
- No oral mucositis, no significant hair thinning reported by patient.
- Hypothyroidism: Remains on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Reports no symptoms of hypothyroidism (no fatigue beyond cabozantinib-related, no cold intolerance, no constipation beyond prior issues). TSH at last check within therapeutic range.
- Weight: Stable at approximately 178 lbs (down approximately 20 lbs from his weight of 198 lbs at initial diagnosis, but stable for the past several months and not declining). Patient attributes baseline weight loss to his initial illness and early treatment course.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1 as noted. Appetite mildly reduced (dysgeusia). Weight stable. No fever, night sweats, or chills.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Dysgeusia grade 1 (persistent metallic/flat taste). No oral ulcers or mucositis. No visual changes. No epistaxis.
Cardiovascular: Hypertension, controlled on current regimen. No chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea on exertion.
Respiratory: No cough, hemoptysis, or dyspnea. Denies pleuritic chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Diarrhea grade 1 as noted, intermittent, managed with dietary measures and PRN loperamide. No nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, melena, or hematochezia. Denies constipation.
Genitourinary: No gross hematuria (resolved since nephrectomy). Urinary frequency slightly increased; no dysuria or hematuria. Stable renal function on labs.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain. No joint pain or swelling. Mild bilateral plantar foot discomfort consistent with chronic HFS grade 1.
Neurological: No headaches, focal weakness, or sensory changes. No cognitive changes.
Endocrine: Hypothyroidism, managed on levothyroxine -- asymptomatic.
Dermatological: HFS grade 1 (mild erythema, no active blistering). No rash.
Hematologic/Lymphatic: No lymph node swelling noted by patient. No bruising or bleeding.
Psychiatric: Coping well. Engaged in survivorship counseling. Anxious about upcoming imaging results, as expected.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
(Established patient -- summarized from prior notes)
1. Metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid differentiation (Stage IV), right kidney primary -- as above.
2. Hypertension -- longstanding, now on combination antihypertensive therapy given TKI use.
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- managed with metformin; no significant glycemic issues reported during cancer treatment course.
4. Immune-related hypothyroidism (secondary to ipilimumab/nivolumab) -- on levothyroxine.
5. Prior right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection -- approximately 15 months ago.

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS:
1. Cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily (on empty stomach, at same time each day)
2. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (on empty stomach in the morning, 30-60 minutes before breakfast)
3. Lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily
4. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily
5. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily with meals
6. Loperamide 2-4 mg orally as needed for diarrhea (PRN)
7. Urea 20% cream (topical) -- applied twice daily to palms and soles
8. Gel cushion insoles (bilateral)

SOCIAL HISTORY: Retired industrial engineer. Former smoker, 30 pack-year history, quit approximately 15 years ago. Denies alcohol use beyond occasional social drink. Married with adult children. Lives at home. Working part-time in consulting capacity. Engaged with oncology team and understands his disease trajectory. Participates in survivorship counseling program.

FAMILY HISTORY: Not updated at this visit (established patient); prior documented history does not reveal hereditary RCC syndromes (no VHL disease, no Birt-Hogg-Dube, no hereditary leiomyomatosis RCC noted in records). His tumor VHL alteration is felt to be somatic.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 134/84 mmHg; heart rate 72 bpm (regular); respiratory rate 16 breaths/min; temperature 37.1 degrees C; SpO2 97% on room air; weight 178.5 lbs; BMI approximately 26.1.

General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Appropriately engaged in conversation regarding his treatment course. Mildly fatigued appearing but functional.

Head and Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx moist. No mucositis or oral ulcers. No thyromegaly. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Right flank surgical scar well-healed, no erythema or induration. No palpable organomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses. No right upper quadrant tenderness. Bowel sounds active and normal.

Extremities: No bilateral lower extremity edema (resolved compared to prior visit when first progressing on nivolumab). Bilateral palmar surfaces: mild diffuse erythema and skin thickening consistent with grade 1 HFS, no active blisters or ulceration. Bilateral plantar surfaces: similar finding -- hyperkeratosis and mild erythema, no open wounds. Nail changes: mild onycholysis of two toenails noted on left foot, consistent with cabozantinib effect.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease is not clinically detectable on exam.

Neurological: Cranial nerves intact. No focal deficits. Gait steady.

Skin: No rash beyond described HFS findings. No jaundice or scleral icterus.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

(Results from today's visit)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.4 x 10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mildly low; likely chronic anemia of cancer/TKI effect; stable compared to prior)
- Hematocrit: 34.2% (low)
- Platelets: 198 x 10^9/L (normal; notably normalized from presenting thrombocytosis of 478 K/uL after nephrectomy and systemic therapy)
- Neutrophils: 3.9 x 10^9/L; Lymphocytes: 1.6 x 10^9/L; NLR approximately 2.4 (improved from baseline NLR 4.2 at diagnosis)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L; Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L; Chloride: 103 mEq/L; Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 22 mg/dL; Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (stable from prior visit; eGFR approximately 38 mL/min/1.73m2, consistent with single-kidney physiology and prior CKD)
- Glucose: 102 mg/dL (fasting, within acceptable range on metformin; HbA1c deferred today, due next month)
- Calcium: 9.4 mg/dL (normalized from elevated 10.9 mg/dL at initial presentation -- reassuring, though largely expected after nephrectomy removing PTHrP-secreting primary)
- Total protein: 6.9 g/dL; Albumin: 3.8 g/dL; Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- AST: 28 U/L; ALT: 32 U/L; Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L (all within normal limits; no hepatotoxicity)
- LDH: 218 U/L (decreased from 287 U/L at initial presentation; favorable trend)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 2.8 mIU/L (within therapeutic range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily)
- Free T4: 1.2 ng/dL (normal)

Urinalysis: Trace protein; no hematuria; no casts. Consistent with single-kidney proteinuria; stable.

Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (performed approximately 3 months ago at 6-month cabozantinib mark):
Pulmonary metastases remain stable -- right lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm, left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm, right upper lobe nodule 0.3 cm, all essentially unchanged over the past several restaging scans. Retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease stable: right common iliac nodal mass 2.2 cm, aortocaval conglomerate 2.5 cm, right obturator node 1.3 cm, right external iliac node 0.8 cm. No new hepatic, pulmonary, or CNS lesions.

Most recent bone scan (performed approximately 3 months ago): No convincing evidence of osseous metastatic disease. The small sclerotic focus in the right iliac bone identified on CT was assessed as most consistent with reactive/degenerative change.

Plan for next restaging imaging is discussed today (see Assessment/Plan below).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Impression: Mr. Kowalski is a 64-year-old gentleman with metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid features, progressed after cytoreductive nephrectomy and ipilimumab/nivolumab-based first-line therapy, now on second-line cabozantinib for approximately 9 months. Best response to cabozantinib has been a partial response in the nodal compartment, with stable pulmonary disease throughout his TKI course. The most recent restaging at 6 months showed stable disease in all compartments. He is tolerating the drug reasonably well at the reduced dose of 40 mg daily, with manageable chronic grade 1 toxicities (HFS, diarrhea, dysgeusia) and well-controlled blood pressure and thyroid function.

Overall, he is doing well from a disease management perspective, 18 months out from initial diagnosis of a biologically aggressive tumor (grade 4, 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, MET amplification, BAP1/PBRM1 co-loss). His performance status has improved back to ECOG 1 and he continues to work part-time and maintain quality of life. This is an encouraging trajectory.

Problem-by-problem assessment and plan:

1. Metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid differentiation -- STABLE DISEASE on cabozantinib 40 mg daily.
   - Patient and I discussed his overall disease course and current status at length. He understands that his disease remains metastatic and not curable with current therapy, but that the goal is to maintain disease control and quality of life for as long as possible.
   - Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. No dose modification warranted given stable disease with manageable toxicity profile at current dose. I did not re-escalate to 60 mg given that we achieved excellent disease control at 40 mg and his toxicities, while managed, would be expected to worsen with dose increase.
   - Proceed with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for third restaging on cabozantinib (approximately 9 months of TKI). I have ordered this scan to be completed within the next 1-2 weeks and we will review results at his next appointment or via portal message if results come in prior to scheduled follow-up.
   - If at next restaging we see disease progression on cabozantinib, the discussion will shift toward third-line options. Potential options would include everolimus or lenvatinib/everolimus combination, or consideration of a clinical trial. His MET amplification and complex genomic landscape may also make him a candidate for investigational options if available.
   - Patient instructed to contact clinic immediately for new or worsening symptoms including new bone pain, new neurological symptoms (headache, visual changes, focal weakness), hemoptysis, or significant worsening of GI symptoms.

2. Hand-foot syndrome (HFS), grade 1, chronic -- stable and manageable.
   - Continue current regimen: urea 20% cream twice daily, protective footwear, gel insoles, avoiding prolonged standing and repetitive trauma to palms.
   - Mild onycholysis noted on left foot toenails today -- patient educated on nail care: keep nails trimmed short, avoid trauma, monitor for secondary infection. No antifungal prophylaxis indicated at this time unless signs of fungal superinfection develop.
   - No dose modification warranted for grade 1 HFS.

3. Diarrhea, grade 1, intermittent -- stable and manageable.
   - Continue PRN loperamide approach. Patient counseled to maintain low-residue, low-fat diet and ensure adequate oral hydration.
   - If diarrhea escalates to grade 2 (4-6 stools above baseline) or becomes constant, patient should contact us promptly for early management and potential dose hold evaluation.

4. Dysgeusia, grade 1 -- chronic, stable.
   - Taste changes are a recognized and often underappreciated side effect of cabozantinib and other TKIs, mediated partly through zinc depletion. Discussed dietary strategies: zinc-containing foods, marinating meats to enhance flavor, avoiding metallic utensils, eating at room temperature. If significant appetite impact progresses leading to weight loss, will consider zinc supplementation (off-label but low-risk) and nutritional oncology referral.
   - Weight stable today, so no urgent intervention indicated.

5. Hypertension -- controlled on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg.
   - Blood pressure today 134/84 mmHg -- adequate control. Continue current antihypertensive regimen without changes.
   - Patient performing home BP monitoring. Instructed to contact us if readings consistently exceed 150/95 mmHg.

6. Immune-related hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily -- well-controlled.
   - TSH 2.8 mIU/L today, within therapeutic range. No dose change needed.
   - Continue levothyroxine; recheck TSH at next routine visit (approximately 3 months).

7. Type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin.
   - Glucose 102 mg/dL today, acceptable. HbA1c to be checked next month as part of diabetes monitoring.
   - Metformin 1000 mg BID continued. No significant glycemic concerns raised on today's visit.
   - Note: Cabozantinib has been associated with hyperglycemia in some patients; will review HbA1c at next visit. Patient maintains adequate glucose control with current regimen.

8. CKD (stage 3b, post-nephrectomy) -- stable.
   - Creatinine stable at 1.8 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 38 mL/min/1.73m2). Trace proteinuria on urinalysis -- consistent with single-kidney physiology.
   - Continue monitoring. Avoid nephrotoxins. Ensure adequate hydration, particularly given ongoing diarrhea.
   - Metformin is used with caution given eGFR <45; at current level of 38 the FDA label suggests reassessment. Will document this issue; given stable renal function and excellent glucose control, I discussed with patient. I am comfortable continuing current dose with close monitoring but will hold metformin if eGFR falls below 30 or with any acute illness causing volume depletion.

9. Quality of life and psychosocial support.
   - Patient is coping well and is engaged with survivorship counseling. He continues to work part-time, which appears to be beneficial for his sense of purpose and well-being.
   - Encouraged ongoing social support and physical activity as tolerated (within limitations imposed by HFS).
   - Discussed the importance of communicating early toxicity concerns and not tolerating side effects in silence.

Return to clinic: 3-4 weeks for restaging CT results review and general follow-up. Sooner if new concerning symptoms develop.

Time spent in face-to-face encounter including counseling and coordination of care: approximately 45 minutes.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Physician, Medical Oncology / Genitourinary Oncology]
[Institution Name]
